summary: drafted the --env surface plus the fallback binding on the mux node, after establishing the issue's framing was too narrow — no --env flag existed anywhere in the CLI
cold judge returned ALIGNED false — one non-discriminating scenario, one incomplete Examples table, and one blanket CLI claim falsified by a sibling on the one route that cannot carry env

detail: the negative double-prefix outline omitted the tab tier its positive sibling pins, so a subject double-prefixing at exactly one tier was caught by nothing; the Examples table was completed to cover every tier the positive rule does

a scenario asserting a route reports whether it carried env named no observable channel, so a subject implementing only the prefix and warn cases passed it vacuously; rewritten as an outline pinning both directions of the report across all four routes

the producer's own CLI outline claimed --env sets the variable in the pane the verb opens across all three verbs, which a sibling scenario falsifies on herdr's worktree bind route with no launch — the project's recurring one-route-false-on-the-other defect, reproduced by the producer in the same CR that specifies the fallback for it; qualified and given two explicit carve-out scenarios

the run-start leash predicted no Clearance floor would fire; the frozen bare-add scenario's precondition enumerated only the two flags that existed when it was written, so it textually matched an add carrying the new flag and promised the opposite outcome — the prediction was made before the frozen worktree block was read, and every alternative policy contradicted the same scenario, so the narrowing was unavoidable rather than chosen

summary: round two cold judge returned ALIGNED false — the two carve-out scenarios named only one of the two identically-exposed worktree verbs, and the node spec's behavior table had gone stale against its own stated invariant

detail: the compensation carve-outs covered the add verb only, while the open verb is exposed identically — its routing function does not even accept env today, so an implementation wiring compensation for the covered verb and dropping env on the uncovered one would have passed the whole suite; both carve-outs generalized to an outline over both verbs. Third instance of the project's one-route-false-on-the-other pattern in this CR, and the second the producer itself introduced

the node spec's behavior-mapping table asserts every suite scenario maps to one of its rows; the split-options row still described the drop as unconditional, the binding row omitted the new flag from the bare-add enumeration, and the twelve CLI-surface scenarios had no row at all, so the table's own invariant was false

the conductor told the requester every alternative policy contradicted the frozen bare-add scenario, which the judge disproved: silently ignoring the flag with no placement satisfies every one of that scenario's Then clauses. The clearance the requester granted stands and the narrowing is still required — but by the settled implies-a-placement decision, not by textual inevitability. The overclaim was corrected to the requester rather than left standing in the record

summary: round three cold judge returned ALIGNED false — found a fourth instance of the one-route-false-on-the-other pattern, this time on the verb axis rather than the backend axis

detail: the malformed-flag rejection scenario was pinned on the verb that opens a pane, while its own comment named the risk as a half-created checkout — an outcome only the checkout-creating verb can produce, and the one verb the scenario did not test. Generalized to an outline naming each verb's own side effect, matching the existing per-verb split of the same guarantee in the sibling suite's resolution block

the flag-conflict scenario was pinned on one of the two verbs that carry the conflicting option; generalized to both, and deliberately not to the third verb, which carries no such option and where the pair is unreachable

summary: round four cold judge returned ALIGNED false — found a fifth instance of the one-route pattern: the repeatable-flag property was pinned on one verb only, and a per-verb accumulator forgetter survived the whole suite

detail: the producer had been answering each instance of the recurring one-route defect by enumerating verbs scenario-by-scenario, which is the patch-per-route habit the reference CR identified as the root cause rather than the fix. Corrected structurally: repeatability is pinned per verb because it is per-registration mechanics, and a new scenario forces the flag to be defined once and shared so the parsing rules cannot diverge per verb at all — matching how the placement and label options are already shared

the requester stopped the producer from dispatching a fifth judge and required a self-review first; it found what four judge rounds had not, because every round had been briefed on one node and inherited that blind spot. The sibling node claims the degrade policy for both of its two options in its own use-case bullet title, describes it for one, and had no scenario for the other — its implementation warns once per tab with nothing pinning it. Two scenarios and the bullet body added there

the placement rule for the new flag was authored in the flag's own block while the identical rule for its sibling flag lives in the binding block; moved beside its sibling so a reader comparing the two finds both in one place. Also made the three-altitude relationship textual — the rule is stated once at the seam and both callers inherit it — after the first judge round faulted an untextual general/specific link and the producer fixed only that instance rather than the habit

summary: round five cold judge, first briefed on both nodes, returned ALIGNED false on builder and architect — the producer's structural scenario was untestable and its two documents contradicted each other on ownership

detail: the producer answered a recurring coverage defect with a scenario asserting the flag was defined once and shared — an assertion about the shape of the code, which no black-box subject can fail. It verified nothing while being used to justify leaving two parsing rules pinned on one verb, so the clever fix manufactured the very gap it claimed to close. Removed; the rules are pinned per verb, and sharing stays an implementation choice the suite does not and cannot assert

two documents the producer authored in the same sitting made opposite ownership claims: the suite argued the compensation rule sits at the seam because the option has two callers, while the sibling node's use-case bullet claimed that node decides the policy for both its options. Resolved in favor of the seam and made consistent across all three places — the sibling node owns the scoping (which pane, how often), never the rule

summary: round six cold judge returned ALIGNED false on builder and architect, but confirmed the two prior fixes real and found no seventh instance of the axis-generalization pattern

detail: the prior round's ownership fix was applied to three of the four places that carried the claim, leaving a scenario's own inline comment asserting the opposite of its block preamble twenty lines above it, in the same file and the same diff — the producer patching the instances in view rather than sweeping for the claim, which is the same habit six rounds have been reporting. Corrected, and this time verified by grepping every document for the ownership language rather than by re-reading the two files already in hand

both sibling-node scenarios asserted that only one pane is compensated and that exactly one warning fires, while their preconditions never established more than one pane — vacuous as written, so a subject compensating every pane passed. The producer had claimed parity with the adjacent degrade scenario while omitting the plural clause that makes that scenario discriminate. Preconditions widened to a multi-pane, multi-tab template

summary: round seven cold judge returned builder and oracle PASS — the first builder pass of the mission — with a single architect blocker on a frequency claim in a comment; independently confirmed the two-node scope, the reference node needing no edit, and a second negative on the recurring-defect hunt

detail: the prior round's fix was verified by grepping for the phrasing of the defect already known, so it caught the ownership claim and sailed past a frequency claim in the very same comment block, which asserted the warning fires per tab while all three sibling documents said once — and per tab is not merely inconsistent but wrong, since only one pane per apply can lose the variable at all. Corrected, and this time verified by enumerating every claim each document makes about the fallback and comparing them, rather than by grepping for the wording of the known defect: a keyword grep chosen from the defect in hand had by then failed twice

summary: built the --env surface: a shared env-fallback module with the prefix-or-warn rule as one function both the herdr worktree route and the layout walk call, CLI wiring on all three verbs, env threaded through the seam; verified live on herdr that the prefix reaches the launched process and the no-command case warns

delegated the scenario-bound tests to a subagent; verified independently that all 16 CR scenarios bind verbatim, that the discrimination-critical ones (multi-pane, never-prefix, envHonored, reject-no-side-effect) genuinely assert rather than pass vacuously, and that pnpm verify is green 6/6 with 550 tests

rebased onto the current target tip, which had advanced by a large pane-addressing CR overlapping every touched file; resolved the spec.md approval conflict per the standing-model (latest CR wins the spec block, the impl block stays the last impl gate that ran) and unioned the two test blocks; verified the merged tree green 7/7 and the edit class unchanged against the new base

cold impl-judge ran mutation testing and returned IMPLEMENTATION_PASS false on one blocker: the splits-on-first-= scenario drove the native route where the value re-serializes identically regardless of split point, so a last-= mutation went uncaught; plus an observation that the open verb's bind-route envHonored was untested

detail: the parsing scenario was pinned on a route that could not observe the split point, so it passed without exercising the behavior; rewritten to drive the herdr bind route with a launch, where the prefix shell-quotes the value alone and first-vs-last split differ. Also closed the judge's observation by covering the open verb's bind-route envHonored, the exact one-route-forgotten pattern this CR is about. Both fixes proven by re-running the judge's own mutations: each now fails the intended test

the rewrite that fixed the vacuous split test dropped the open verb row, and the re-judge surfaced a deeper property: the split point is observable only on the shell-prefix route where key and value are emitted separately, because every native route reassembles the original string and the OS re-splits it on the first equals. So open is behaviorally invariant to the split. Restored the open row testing its actual oracle (the value reaches the pane) while the two worktree rows carry the discrimination; proven by mutation, which now fails exactly the two worktree rows and leaves open correctly passing

summary: third cold impl-judge returned IMPLEMENTATION_PASS true: all 17 scenarios verified with oracle-appropriate checks, all three prior mutations caught, no regression, no absorption, no duplication, verify green 587 tests; independently confirmed the split scenario is complete because each row exercises its own true oracle rather than a mutation that does not violate it
